narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 and LSAT score of 33, both below the typical averages of 3.21 and 36.70 seen among past students in our dataset. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 44.8%—a risk level that unfortunately crossed our threshold for approval, leading to the rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by three key factors from your academic record, ranked by their influence on the outcome. Your undergraduate GPA had the strongest positive sway toward passing, as it suggested a solid foundation despite being a bit lower than average, pulling the model's view in your favor more than any other element. Next, your LSAT score also nudged the prediction toward success, showing the model recognized your testing potential even if it wasn't at the top tier. However, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3—which places you in the lower third of your peers' performance that year—had a notable counteracting effect, pushing the overall assessment toward a higher failure risk compared to students who ranked higher. In contrast to typical applicants who often have stronger decile rankings and higher test scores aligning more closely with bar passage success rates, your profile showed some promising elements but was held back by that later academic dip. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man from a supportive background aiming for full-time study—many with similar starts have succeeded by addressing weak spots. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your upper-level grades through targeted coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience and dedication. Reapplying after these improvements could shift the model's prediction significantly in your favor, opening doors to law school. You're clearly driven, and with focused steps, you can rewrite this story.
